bmrConceptModal.title: What is BMR?
bmrConceptModal.subtitle: Basal Metabolic Rate
bmrConceptModal.descriptionText: BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) is the amount of energy the body needs at rest to maintain all vital functions such as breathing, circulation, organ function and the basic processes of cells. It is the body's "baseline expenditure".
bmrConceptModal.factor1Plain: More muscle mass — muscles require more energy than fat even at rest.
bmrConceptModal.factor2Plain: Higher body weight — larger bodies need more energy to run basic functions.
bmrConceptModal.factor3Plain: Higher body temperature and active metabolism — for example during fever or rapid cell turnover.
bmrConceptModal.factor4Plain: Younger age — children and young people often have a higher BMR.
bmrConceptModal.factor5Plain: Genetics — some people naturally have a faster or slower basal energy expenditure.
bmrConceptModal.factor6Plain: Hormonal factors — e.g. higher levels of thyroid hormone increase BMR.

palConceptModal.title: What is PAL?
palConceptModal.subtitle: Physical Activity Level
palConceptModal.descriptionText: PAL stands for Physical Activity Level and describes your average energy expenditure relative to your basal metabolic rate (BMR). The higher your PAL, the more active you are assumed to be, and the higher your estimated calorie requirement (TDEE).
palConceptModal.howItWorksPara: The PAL value is multiplied by your BMR to obtain your total daily energy requirement (TDEE). Different PAL systems use different methods to calculate this value based on your activity level.
palConceptModal.whyDifferentTitle: Why are there different PAL systems?
palConceptModal.whyDifferentPara1: Different PAL systems have been developed for different purposes and target groups. Some are research-based and conservative, while others are more detailed and take into account specific types of training and everyday activity.
palConceptModal.whyDifferentPara2: Choose the PAL system that best matches your lifestyle and training level. Click the info icon next to each system to read more about its advantages and limitations.

palTable.customPALHint: Enter your own PAL value if you know it. Common values range between 1.2 (sedentary) and 2.2 (very active).
palTable.customPALWarning: Unusual PAL value — normal range is 1.2–2.2

noProfile.title: No active profile
noProfile.description: You need a profile to use the genetic muscle potential tool.

header.title: Genetic Muscle Potential
header.description: Calculate your maximum muscle mass based on height and body structure

maleOnly.title: Genetic Muscle Potential is for men only
maleOnly.description: These models and frameworks (Martin Berkhan's model, Casey Butt's model, Alan Aragon's framework and Lyle McDonald's framework) are all developed and validated for men. They do not account for women's physiology and hormonal differences, meaning the results would not be accurate or useful for women.

addBodyFatTip.title: Tip: Add body fat percentage
addBodyFatTip.description: To see your current progress towards genetic potential and how many kg of lean mass you have left to build, we recommend entering your body fat percentage in your profile. The calculations work without this, but you get more insightful information with body fat percentage.
addBodyFatTip.link: Go to Profile to enter body fat percentage

infoCard.title: About Genetic Muscle Potential
infoCard.description: These models estimate your maximum natural muscle mass. Results vary between formulas depending on what factors they consider. No formula is 100% accurate, but they provide a useful guideline for realistic goals.

geneticPotential.exceedingPotential: Above theoretical max for lean mass:
geneticPotential.capacityAtCurrentBF: Estimated maximum capacity at current {{bf}}% body fat

caseyButtInfo.para1: Casey Butt is a researcher (PhD) and powerlifter who published a comprehensive empirical model in the 2000s for estimating genetic muscle potential in natural athletes. The model is based on analysis of historical data from natural bodybuilders and strength athletes over several decades, particularly from periods when performance-enhancing drugs were uncommon.
caseyButtInfo.para2: Unlike simpler height- and weight-based formulas, Butt's model accounts for individual skeletal structure through wrist and ankle circumference measurements. These are used to estimate frame size and muscle potential. The model also provides separate calculations for maximum body measurements (e.g. arms, chest, thighs) at genetic potential.
caseyButtInfo.para3: The model is empirical and observation-based, not a formally scientifically validated study, but is often used as a reference for realistic upper limits of natural muscle development, not as a statistical average model.
caseyButtInfo.step1Vars: H = height (inches), W = wrist (inches), A = ankle (inches), BF% = body fat
caseyButtInfo.easygainerVars: W = wrist, A = ankle, H = height (inches)
caseyButtInfo.source: Source: Butt, C. "Your Muscular Potential: How to Predict Your Maximum Muscular Bodyweight and Measurements" weightrainer.net (originally published early 2000s, updated 2009)
caseyButtInfo.ambiguityNote: There is ambiguity with this model. Casey Butt does not strictly specify which body fat percentage to use, but describes: "%bf = The body fat percentage at which you want to predict your maximum lean body mass". This leaves it open to interpretation and the model can therefore be used in two different ways:
caseyButtInfo.alternativesLabel:
Option 1: Standardised genetic reference
Option 2: State-dependent lean body mass

caseyButtInputs.sectionTitle: Circumference measurements for the Casey Butt model
caseyButtInputs.descWithData: These measurements are used to calculate skeletal structure and muscle potential. Values are pre-filled from your active measurement card.
caseyButtInputs.descWithoutData: These measurements are used to calculate skeletal structure and muscle potential. Enter your measurements manually or save them in body composition.
caseyButtInputs.wristInfoDesc: Measured on the palm side of the styloid processes, where the forearm is narrowest.

caseyButtMethod.sectionTitle: Choose calculation method for the Casey Butt model
caseyButtMethod.standardDesc: Calculates MLBM using 10% body fat as a reference, then converted to your actual body fat percentage. Comparable between individuals and based on Casey Butt's original data.
caseyButtMethod.personalizedDesc: Shows how much lean body mass your body can carry at your current body fat percentage, including fluids and glycogen.
caseyButtMethod.missingBodyFat: ⚠️ Body fat missing — using 10% as fallback

standardModal.title: Standardised genetic reference
standardModal.description: This method calculates your maximum genetic potential in two steps:
standardModal.step1: MLBM (Maximum Lean Body Mass) is calculated using 10% body fat as a standardised reference point
standardModal.step2Label: converted to your actual body fat percentage
standardModal.step2Post: to show your maximum weight at your current body fat
standardModal.step3: Casey Butt's analysis was based on approximately 300 drug-free bodybuilding champions and strength athletes from 1947–2010. These athletes were in a lean, stable condition (~8–10% body fat), which is why 10% is used as the reference point for the MLBM calculation.
standardModal.step4: By using 10% as a fixed reference in the MLBM formula, but then converting to your actual body fat percentage, you get a genetically meaningful result that is also relevant to your current condition.
standardModal.pro1: Genetically meaningful — MLBM based on a standardised reference
standardModal.pro2: Comparable between individuals — everyone uses the same 10% reference
standardModal.pro4: Relevant to your current condition — shows weight at your actual body fat
standardModal.con1: May be harder to understand (two-step calculation)
standardModal.con2: Requires knowledge of your body fat percentage for best results

personalizedModal.title: State-dependent lean body mass
personalizedModal.para1: The result will show how much lean body mass your body can carry in this condition. It also includes fluids, glycogen and other non-permanent components.
personalizedModal.para2: Here the user's %BF is used directly in MLBM (maximum lean body mass). MBW (maximum body weight) and MBBW (maximum bulked body weight) are then calculated from this.
personalizedModal.pro1: Fully correct according to the formula
personalizedModal.con1: MLBM becomes state-dependent (MLBM increases when %BF increases)
personalizedModal.con3: Less suitable as a genetic ceiling

berkhanInfo.para1: Martin Berkhan is the founder of the Leangains method and an early advocate of intermittent fasting in the fitness world. In the article "Maximum Muscular Potential of Drug-Free Athletes" he presents a simple model for estimating maximum muscle mass in natural (drug-free) athletes.
berkhanInfo.para2: The model is based on observations of elite bodybuilders and competitive athletes at very low body fat percentages, primarily around 5–6% for men. Berkhan suggests that maximum competition weight in peak condition can be estimated using the formula:
berkhanInfo.para3: He emphasises that this value represents an upper practical limit for natural athletes in extreme competition shape, rather than an average or a guaranteed goal. Berkhan also notes that individual genetics, body structure and height may justify minor adjustments around this estimate (typically ±2–4 kg).
berkhanInfo.para4: The model is empirical and based on practical observations, not formal scientific validation, and should therefore be seen as a rough guideline rather than an exact physiological law.
berkhanInfo.source: Source: Berkhan, M. "Maximum Muscular Potential of Drug-Free Athletes" leangains.com (2008-2010) (updated 31 Dec 2010)

caseyButtMissing.title: Circumference measurements required for the Casey Butt model
caseyButtMissing.description: To calculate your results with Casey Butt's model you need to enter circumference measurements for wrist and ankle in the fields above. These measurements can either be pre-filled from your active measurement card in Body Composition, or you can enter them manually.

resultCard.targetWeightsTitle: Estimated maximum genetic potential for body weight
resultCard.targetWeightsSubtitle: Max weight at different body fat percentages

lyleMcDonald.para1: Lyle McDonald is an author and analyst in nutrition and strength training who since the late 1990s has published extensive material on muscle building, energy balance and training adaptation. In the article "What's My Genetic Muscular Potential?" he discusses how muscle growth gradually slows with increased training age and approaches an individual genetic upper limit.
lyleMcDonald.para2: McDonald's reasoning is based on a synthesis of scientific literature, practical observations and comparisons between beginners, intermediates and advanced trainees. Unlike quantitative models for maximum muscle mass, he presents no specific calculation procedure, but offers a theoretical framework for understanding likely development trajectories and realistic expectations over time.
lyleMcDonald.source: Source: McDonald, L. What's My Genetic Muscular Potential? BodyRecomposition.com
lyleMcDonald.tableSubtitle: Based on "proper" training with progressive overload
lyleMcDonald.footNote: Note: These values assume correct training, nutrition and recovery

alanAragon.para1: Alan Aragon is a nutritionist and coach, and founder of Alan Aragon's Research Review (AARR). In his publications, including the article "Girth Control: The Science of Fat Loss and Muscle Gain" (2007), he discusses how muscle building and body composition change in relation to training level, experience and time.
alanAragon.para2: Aragon's perspective focuses on realistic expectations and adaptation rates rather than establishing absolute genetic limits. He describes how the potential for muscle gain is greatest in beginners and successively decreases in more advanced trainees. The work thus functions as a conceptual framework for time-dependent muscle growth, not as a numerical model for maximum muscle mass.
alanAragon.source: Source: Aragon, A. Girth Control: The Science of Fat Loss and Muscle Gain (2007), Alan Aragon's Research Review
alanAragon.tableSubtitle: Percentage of total body weight per month
alanAragon.footNote: Note: Assess for yourself where you are based on your actual training experience

bodyType.upperHardDesc: Thinner skeletal structure — requires more focus for upper body development
bodyType.upperEasyDesc: Thicker skeletal structure — easier to build upper body muscles
bodyType.lowerHardDesc: Thinner skeletal structure — requires more focus for lower body development
bodyType.lowerEasyDesc: Thicker skeletal structure — easier to build lower body muscles

maxMeasurements.chestInfoDesc: Raise your arms and place the tape measure around the upper part of the torso, below the armpits, before lowering the arms to the sides. Chest circumference is measured horizontally at exhalation and in a relaxed state.
maxMeasurements.bicepsInfoDesc: Measured in a flexed position at the largest circumference, with the arm raised forward and elbow at 45°.
maxMeasurements.forearmsInfoDesc: With a clenched fist and arm extended, measure the circumference at the largest point, usually closer to the elbow.
maxMeasurements.neckInfoDesc:
According to Topend Sports: Immediately above the Adam's apple.
According to Casey Butt: Just below the Adam's apple, at the narrowest point.

The person should keep their head upright and look straight ahead.
maxMeasurements.thighsInfoDesc: Measured at the midpoint of the outer thigh, halfway between the greater trochanter and the lateral tibial condyle.
maxMeasurements.calvesInfoDesc: Measured at the largest circumference, with muscles relaxed.

palFields.customPALHint: Enter your own PAL value if you know it
palFields.customPALWarning: Unusual PAL value — normal range is 1.0–2.5

energyGoalTable.title: Energy goal - Overview
energyGoalTable.invalidTdee: Invalid TDEE value. Please calculate your TDEE first.
energyGoalTable.maintainDesc: For weight stabilisation and maintenance of current weight
energyGoalTable.gainDesc: For muscle building and weight gain · ~{{kgMin}}–{{kgMax}} kg/week
energyGoalTable.lossCautiousDesc: Slow weight loss, best for preserving muscle mass · ~{{kgMin}}–{{kgMax}} kg/week
energyGoalTable.lossNormalDesc: Balanced weight loss with good energy levels · ~{{kgMin}}–{{kgMax}} kg/week
energyGoalTable.lossAggressiveDesc: Rapid weight loss, short periods only · ~{{kgMin}}–{{kgMax}} kg/week

mifflinStJeor.name: Mifflin-St Jeor equation
mifflinStJeor.description: One of the most validated and widely used formulas for calculating resting metabolic rate in healthy adults.
pros: Validated across different BMI ranges — consistently performs well in normal-weight, overweight, and obese individuals
Multiple meta-analyses (e.g. Frankenfield et al., 2005) show that Mifflin-St Jeor is the most accurate RMR predictor for healthy non-obese and obese adults in the US and Western populations
cons: Like many predictive equations, it does not account for lean body mass, so it may underestimate RMR in highly muscular individuals (e.g. athletes)
mifflinStJeor.measurements:
Weight in kg, height in cm, age in years
sex = +5 for men, −161 for women

cunningham.name: Cunningham (Katch and McArdle) equation
cunningham.description: Developed by Cunningham and popularised by Katch and McArdle in their textbook on exercise physiology.
pros: Considered more accurate for individuals with low body fat percentage or high muscle mass, such as athletes
Accounts for the fact that muscle tissue requires more energy to maintain compared to fat cells
cons: Requires knowledge of your body fat percentage to be used

oxfordHenry.name: Oxford/Henry equation
oxfordHenry.description: Unlike the older Schofield equations (used by FAO/WHO/UNU), the Oxford equations excluded biased Italian data that had unusually high BMR values.
pros: Provides lower, more accurate BMR estimates for many global populations
cons: May underestimate needs in populations where physical activity level or muscle mass is high
oxfordHenry.measurements_male: Weight in kg, height in m
oxfordHenry.measurements_female: Weight in kg, height in m

schofield.name: Schofield (FAO/WHO/UNU) equation
schofield.description: Developed by W.N. Schofield as part of a study commissioned by the FAO and WHO to standardise energy requirements worldwide.
pros: Based on 7,173 subjects from 114 studies — one of the most comprehensive BMR datasets available in the 1980s
Remains one of the most widely used equations for estimating energy requirements, particularly in clinical and public health settings
cons: Overestimation of BMR in women over 18, adults with lower body weight, and people from tropical regions
Overrepresentation of Italian subjects (47% of the data), who had higher BMR than average
Underrepresentation of tropical populations (e.g. India, Philippines, Brazil), leading to systematic overestimation of BMR in many groups

revisedHarrisBenedict.name: Revised Harris-Benedict (Roza and Shizgal) equation
revisedHarrisBenedict.description: In 1984, the original Harris-Benedict equations were revised with new data by Roza and Shizgal.
pros: Comparisons with actual energy expenditure showed the revised equations to be more accurate than the original
cons: Less accurate for individuals with atypical body composition (e.g. very lean or very obese)
In several validation studies, the Mifflin-St Jeor equation has outperformed Roza and Shizgal in predicting BMR, particularly in modern populations with a higher prevalence of overweight and obesity
revisedHarrisBenedict.measurements_male: Weight in kg, height in cm, age in years
revisedHarrisBenedict.measurements_female: Weight in kg, height in cm, age in years

originalHarrisBenedict.name: Original Harris-Benedict equation
originalHarrisBenedict.description: The Harris-Benedict formula was groundbreaking in 1918 and laid the foundation for metabolic research. The 1919 publication is more comprehensive and is often cited as the foundational work for estimating basal metabolic rate (BMR).
pros: Historically important work that laid the foundation for BMR calculations
cons: Tends to overestimate BMR, especially in women and those with lower lean body mass — often overestimates by 4–21% depending on the individual
Compared to newer formulas such as Mifflin-St Jeor (1990), Harris-Benedict performs worse
originalHarrisBenedict.measurements_male: Weight in kg, height in cm, age in years
originalHarrisBenedict.measurements_female: Weight in kg, height in cm, age in years

macroFactorStandard.name: MacroFactor Standard equation
macroFactorStandard.description: Based on the Oxford/Henry equation and the Cunningham equation. Accounts for non-linear scaling of metabolism.
pros: Accounts for the fact that metabolism does not scale linearly with body weight — someone weighing twice as much does not necessarily burn twice as many calories
Modern formula developed with consideration of current research data
cons: Relatively new formula with limited long-term validation
macroFactorStandard.measurements:
Weight in kg, height in cm, age in years
age factor = 1.96 (≤60 years), 4.9 (>60 years)
sex factor = 0 for men, 1 for women

macroFactorFFM.name: MacroFactor FFM equation
macroFactorFFM.description: Variant of the MacroFactor equation that uses fat-free mass (FFM) for more precise calculations.
pros: Better than "Standard" if you know your body fat percentage
Accounts for non-linear scaling of metabolism
cons: Requires knowledge of your body fat percentage
macroFactorFFM.measurements:
FFM = fat-free mass (kg)
FM = fat mass (kg)
age factor = 1.1 (≤60 years), 2.75 (>60 years)

macroFactorAthlete.name: MacroFactor Athlete equation
macroFactorAthlete.description: Specially adapted for athletes who train intensively for at least seven hours per week. Athletes have a higher RMR because they tend to be leaner and have more muscle mass than non-athletes.
pros: Specifically designed for athletes who spend at least seven hours per week in intensive training

Accounts for the fact that the mass of highly metabolic organs (particularly the heart, kidneys, and liver) in athletes scales more strongly with overall body size than the relationship observed in non-athletes
cons: Requires knowledge of your body fat percentage
Only suitable for individuals with very high training volume (7+ hours of intensive training per week)

fitnessStuffPodcast.name: Fitness Stuff Podcast equation
fitnessStuffPodcast.description: Based on the Cunningham equation and the Mifflin-St Jeor equation. This equation uses the Cunningham equation as a base to estimate RMR, but combines the sex component from Mifflin-St Jeor.
pros: Combines strengths from both Cunningham (fat-free mass) and Mifflin-St Jeor (sex-specific)
cons: Requires knowledge of your body fat percentage
fitnessStuffPodcast.measurements:
FFM = fat-free mass (kg)
sex factor = +5 for men, −161 for women
age factor = 1.0 (≤60 years), 0.9 (>60 years)

faoWhoUnu.name: FAO/WHO/UNU-based PAL values
faoWhoUnu.description: This system is best suited to the Schofield formula as they were developed within the same project. The system is based on international research and measurements of actual energy expenditure.
pros: Based on international research and measurements of actual energy expenditure
Stable, conservative, and reliable for most people
Consistent with dietitian and official health authority recommendations
Well established in scientific research
cons: Less flexible for training volume and intensity
Does not account for individual variations in exercise
May underestimate requirements for very active individuals
bestFor: Those who want a conservative, research-based estimate
Beginners looking for a reliable baseline
People following official dietary recommendations
Those using the Schofield formula for BMR

damnripped.name: DAMNRIPPED PAL values
damnripped.description: A detailed system that combines daily activity and training intensity for a more precise calculation. More based on fitness practice than research standardisation.
pros: Very detailed — combines daily activity + training intensity
Works well for people who exercise regularly
Accounts for training intensity, not just frequency
Flexible adaptation to individual training level
cons: Not research-standardised; more based on fitness practice
Risk of overestimating activity level
Requires good self-awareness about training intensity
Can give values that are too high if not realistic
bestFor: Active exercisers who know their intensity level
People who want more detailed control over the calculation
Those who train regularly with varying intensity
Experienced users who can assess their training realistically

proPhysique.name: Pro Physique PAL values
proPhysique.description: A coaching-based system that clearly separates daily activity (base PAL) from training energy. Provides flexible fine-tuning based on training volume.
pros: Clearly separates daily activity (base PAL) from training energy
Very useful for people who strength train and follow programmes
Provides flexible fine-tuning based on training volume
Accounts for both frequency and duration of training
cons: Base PAL is intentionally low and may seem confusing
Requires the user to know how intensive their training sessions are
Not a classic PAL system — more of a coaching model
Can be complex for beginners
bestFor: Strength training enthusiasts who follow structured programmes
People who want to separate base activity from training
Those who have a good grasp of training volume and intensity
Experienced trainees seeking precision in their calculations

fitnessStuff.name: Fitness Stuff PAL values
fitnessStuff.description: A unique model that accounts for both exercise (EAT) and daily movement (NEAT). The system weighs in both weekly training hours and average step count, where NEAT has a greater impact on calorie expenditure than structured exercise.
pros: Very practical and personalised — includes both training and daily steps
Better for active people with varying lifestyles
Gives higher precision if the user knows their steps and training hours
Accounts for NEAT which has a large impact on TDEE
Structured exercise and daily movement are weighted separately
cons: Requires more input from the user
The calculation is more complex
May overestimate TDEE if step data is not accurate
Requires careful tracking of step count
bestFor: Active people who track their daily steps
Those with varying activity patterns
People with access to a step counter or smartwatch
Those who want the most detailed calorie estimate
Users who understand the difference between NEAT and EAT

basicInternet.name: Basic internet PAL values
basicInternet.description: Based on old Harris-Benedict multipliers that have been simplified and spread online. A quick and simple system used in many online TDEE calculators.
pros: Quick and easy to understand
Used in many online TDEE calculators (especially those using Revised Harris-Benedict)
Good for beginners who want a quick estimate
Low learning curve
cons: Does not originate from modern scientific PAL measurements
Can both over- and underestimate TDEE significantly depending on the person
Less accurate than modern research-based systems
Does not account for individual variations
bestFor: Beginners who want a quick and simple estimate
Those using Revised Harris-Benedict for BMR
People looking for a basic starting point
Those who want a simple system with few choices

advanced.name: Advanced PAL system
advanced.subtitle: MET-based energy model • Type: Bottom-up TDEE
descriptionBlocks: This system calculates total daily energy expenditure (TDEE) by modelling the body's main energy components rather than using a fixed activity multiplier. The model is based on the established breakdown of energy expenditure into basal metabolism (BMR/RMR), activity-related energy expenditure (NEAT + EAT), and the thermic effect of food (TEF):
This breakdown is widely used in energy metabolism research.

NEAT (Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis) is here divided into several sub-components to better reflect actual daily activity: walking and steps, standing activity, and household and work-related movements. These are estimated using MET values (Metabolic Equivalent of Task) from the Compendium of Physical Activities.

Metabolic Equivalent of Task (MET) is a measure of energy expenditure where 1 MET corresponds to the body's energy expenditure at rest (≈ basal metabolism). When physical activity is expressed in MET values, these therefore include both resting expenditure and the additional energy the activity requires. For example, 3.8 MET means the body expends 3.8 times as much energy as at rest. In the formula TDEE = BMR + NEAT + EAT + TEF, BMR (basal metabolism) already represents the body's energy expenditure at rest. To avoid counting resting expenditure twice, the energy from physical activity must therefore be calculated as only the extra energy above rest. This is done by using MET − 1 instead of the full MET value. Using MET − 1 ensures the model is physiologically correct and that each component of the energy balance represents a unique part of the body's energy expenditure, without overlap.

The model also includes a SPA factor (Spontaneous Physical Activity) representing biological variation in spontaneous movements such as postural adjustments, small shifts and fidgeting. Research shows that this type of activity can contribute to large individual differences in energy expenditure between people with similar body composition.
Total energy expenditure is calculated as:
TEF constitutes approximately 10% of TDEE. To avoid calculating TEF separately, TDEE can be solved directly by adjusting for these 10%:
The final PAL value (Physical Activity Level) is then calculated as:
By modelling each component separately, the system can provide a more individualised estimate of energy expenditure than traditional PAL multipliers.
pros: More individualised estimate than traditional PAL factors
Based on established energy physiology (BMR + NEAT + EAT + TEF)
Uses MET values from scientific activity compendiums
Accounts for both training and daily movement
Enables estimation of energy expenditure from multiple activity sources
cons: Requires more input than simpler PAL systems
Self-reported activity can introduce uncertainty
MET values represent population averages and may differ from the individual's actual energy expenditure
NEAT shows large individual variation that is difficult to measure exactly
The result should therefore be seen as an estimate rather than an exact measurement
bestFor: Users who want a more detailed estimate of energy expenditure
People with a step counter or smartwatch
Those who exercise regularly and can estimate training intensity
People with varying activity levels between different days
Users who want to understand how different activities affect their energy requirements

bmr.title: Basal metabolism (BMR / RMR)
blocks: Basal metabolic rate (BMR) is the energy the body requires to maintain basic physiological functions such as breathing, blood circulation, and cellular processes. The most important determinant of BMR is fat-free mass, which is estimated to explain around 80% of the variation in basal metabolism.

In people with low physical activity, BMR typically constitutes approximately 60% of total energy expenditure, and even more in individuals with very low movement activity such as sedated and mechanically ventilated patients in intensive care units.

BMR is measured under strictly controlled laboratory conditions: after sleep, in a fasted state, at rest, and in a thermoneutral environment. In practical settings, resting metabolic rate (RMR) is more commonly used, which unlike BMR represents energy expenditure at rest measured at any time of day and can differ from BMR by up to 10%.
The gold standard for measurement is indirect calorimetry, where oxygen uptake and carbon dioxide production are converted to energy expenditure via Weir's equation (1949):
Where VO₂ and VCO₂ are measured in ml/min.
When indirect calorimetry is not available, predictive equations such as Mifflin-St Jeor, Harris-Benedict, or Cunningham are used, which have lower precision at the individual level.
refs: von Loeffelholz, C., & Birkenfeld, A. L. (2022). Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis in Human Energy Homeostasis. In: Endotext [Internet]. South Dartmouth (MA): MDText.com.
Weir, J. B. de V. (1949). New methods for calculating metabolic rate with special reference to protein metabolism. Journal of Physiology, 109(1–2), 1–9.

neat.title: NEAT (Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis)
blocks: NEAT encompasses all energy expenditure from body movements that are not planned exercise or resting metabolism.

In normally active individuals, NEAT can constitute approximately 15–30% of TDEE, but variation is large. In very inactive individuals, NEAT can be as low as 6–10%, while in very active individuals it can exceed 50% of total energy expenditure.

NEAT is also the most variable component of energy expenditure between individuals with similar body weight and BMI. Therefore, NEAT is of particular interest for weight regulation and fat mass — small differences in spontaneous activity can over years produce considerable energy differences.
In the model, NEAT is divided into three sub-components: locomotion, posture, and other spontaneous movements, including fidgeting (small, often unconscious movements).
refs: Tzeravini E, Tentolouris A, Kokkinos A, Tentolouris N, Katsilambros N. Diet induced thermogenesis, older and newer data with emphasis on obesity and diabetes mellitus - A narrative review. Metabol Open. 2024 Jun 5;22:100291.
von Loeffelholz C, Birkenfeld AL. Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis in Human Energy Homeostasis. In: Feingold KR, et al., editors. Endotext [Internet]. MDText.com, Inc.; 2000-.

neatSteps.title: NEAT_steps (walking and steps)
blocks: Practical estimates show that a person of approximately 70 kg burns around 40 kcal per 1,000 steps (≈0.04 kcal/step). Energy expenditure per step is roughly proportional to body weight and is also affected by walking pace.
A simplified model:

Tempo_factor represents walking speed based on MET values:
Energy expenditure during walking increases with body weight and walking pace, which can be estimated using MET values (Ainsworth et al., 2011) where — moderate pace, 4.5–5.5 km/h, has a MET value of 3.8. — the current MET value is divided by this.
This typically lands between:

The model is a practical estimate that gives a rough picture of energy expenditure during daily movement. There is no standardised peer-reviewed formula that uses exactly this calculation — it is based on established relationships between body weight, walking speed, and energy expenditure described in MET compendiums and research on step-based energy metabolism.
refs: Bassett, D. R., Jr., et al. (2016). Step counting: a review… Sports Medicine, 47(7), 1303–1315.
Ainsworth, B. E., et al. (2011). 2011 Compendium of Physical Activities.
Verywell Fit – Steps to Calories Converter
Omni Calculator – Steps to Calories

neatStanding.title: NEAT_standing (standing activity)
blocks: Standing increases energy expenditure compared to sitting because muscle tone and blood circulation are higher and postural stabilisation requires more energy.

A meta-analysis (Saeidifard et al., 2018) shows that standing on average increases energy expenditure by approximately 0.15 kcal/minute (~9 kcal/hour) compared to sitting. However, the analysis does not account for body weight. A lighter person and a heavier person get the same NEAT_standing in kcal, which is not physiologically correct.

For a weight-adjusted estimate, MET values from the Compendium of Physical Activities can be used, where standing gives a MET value 1.3 times higher than the value for sitting. To include body weight, a MET-based model is proposed:

The meta-analysis value (≈9 kcal/hour) is a simple average suitable for popular science estimates, while the MET-based method accounts for the individual's body weight and can be integrated with other NEAT components such as steps, household work, and small movements. This method is therefore more useful in practical NEAT calculations, while the meta-analysis value serves as a rule of thumb for average individuals.
refs: Saeidifard F, et al. Differences of energy expenditure while sitting versus standing: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Eur J Prev Cardiol. 2018;25(5):522–538.
Ainsworth BE, et al. 2011 Compendium of Physical Activities: A second update of codes and MET values.
Levine JA. Non‑exercise activity thermogenesis (NEAT): environment and biology. Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab. 2004;286(5):E675‑E685.

neatHousehold.title: NEAT_household (household and work)
blocks: NEAT_household covers everyday activities such as cleaning, cooking, and other manual chores.
Energy expenditure is estimated using MET values:
MET values represent population averages and do not fully account for individual differences in work pace, technique, load, or efficiency.

Self-reporting of daily activity is a significant source of error. Activities are often forgotten or underestimated, time estimates are uncertain, errors in reported body weight affect the result linearly. In practical use, the total uncertainty in NEAT_household can be estimated at ±20–30% with self-assessment.
refs: Ainsworth, B. E., et al. (2011; 2024). Compendium of Physical Activities.
Levine, J. A. (2004). Non-exercise activity thermogenesis (NEAT): environment and biology. Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab.

spa.title: Spontaneous physical activity (SPA)
blocks: Spontaneous Physical Activity (SPA) encompasses small, low-intensity, often unconscious movements such as postural adjustments, fidgeting, and small shifts.

Experimental studies using indirect calorimetry show that NEAT exhibits large inter-individual variation. In a controlled overfeeding study, the change in NEAT varied between −98 and +692 kcal/day between individuals with similar body composition (Levine et al., 1999). Reviews report that total differences in NEAT between individuals can amount to several hundred kilocalories per day, and in some cases over 1,000 kcal/day (Levine, 2002; 2004).
These differences include variation in spontaneous movement and postural activity, although SPA is not always separated from other NEAT components.
Since SPA is difficult to measure directly, it can be represented in energy models as an adjustment factor:
A conservative range:
The range 0.95–1.15 is based on empirically observed variation in NEAT and is not a standardised parameter in the literature, but a simplified representation of biological variation.
refs: Levine JA, Eberhardt NL, Jensen MD. Role of nonexercise activity thermogenesis in resistance to fat gain in humans. Science. 1999;283(5399):212–214.
Levine JA. Nonexercise activity thermogenesis. Proc Nutr Soc. 2002;61(3):371–378.
Levine JA. Non-exercise activity thermogenesis (NEAT): environment and biology. Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab. 2004;286(5):E675–E685.

eat.title: EAT (Exercise Activity Thermogenesis)
blocks: EAT represents energy expenditure from planned physical exercise.
It can be estimated using MET values:
Weekly training volume can be divided by 7 to obtain a daily average.

EAT typically constitutes a small proportion of total energy expenditure in sedentary individuals (≈ <5%), but can increase significantly with high training volume and amount to approximately 15–30% or more in very active individuals and elite athletes (Westerterp, 2013; Levine, 2005).

MET values are population-based and may differ from the individual's actual energy expenditure. Post-exercise oxygen consumption (EPOC) occurs mainly after high-intensity exercise and generally contributes a smaller share of total exercise energy at typical training sessions (LaForgia et al., 2006).
refs: Ainsworth BE et al. (2011). 2011 Compendium of Physical Activities. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 43(8):1575–1581.
LaForgia J, Withers RT, Gore CJ. (2006). Effects of exercise intensity and duration on the excess post-exercise oxygen consumption. J Sports Sci. 24(12):1247–1264.
Westerterp KR. (2013). Physical activity and physical activity induced energy expenditure in humans: measurement, determinants, and effects. Front Physiol. 4:90.
Levine JA. (2005). Measurement of energy expenditure. Public Health Nutr. 8(7A):1123–1132.

tef.title: TEF (Thermic Effect of Food)
blocks: TEF is the energy required for digestion, absorption, and metabolism of nutrients.
During energy balance, TEF averages approximately 10% of energy intake, with a typical range of 8–15% depending on meal composition.
It is more correct to express TEF as a proportion of energy intake rather than of TEE, since TEF depends directly on the amount and type of energy consumed.
TEF varies between macronutrients:

The higher thermic effect of protein is due to the energy-demanding metabolism of amino acids, including protein synthesis and urea formation. Consequently, meals with higher protein content generally produce a somewhat higher postprandial energy expenditure compared to fat- or carbohydrate-rich meals, given the same amount of energy. Inter-individual variation occurs where body composition, insulin resistance, and metabolic health, among others, play a role.

These ranges are based on indirect calorimetry and compilations of controlled meal studies (Westerterp, 2004; Halton & Hu, 2004). This model assumes an average TEF of 10%, which is integrated into the TDEE equation.
refs: Kinabo JL, Durnin JV. Thermic effect of food in man: effect of meal composition, and energy content. Br J Nutr. 1990;64:37–44.
Westerterp KR. (2004). Diet induced thermogenesis. Nutr Metab (Lond). 1:5.
von Loeffelholz C, Birkenfeld AL. (2022). Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis in Human Energy Homeostasis. In: Endotext [Internet]. MDText.com.

customPAL.name: Custom PAL value
customPAL.description: Lets you enter your own PAL value based on your own measurements, experience, or recommendations from a nutritionist/trainer. Provides maximum flexibility but requires knowledge of what constitutes a realistic PAL value.
pros: Maximum flexibility and personalisation
Perfect if you have tracked data over time
Useful if you follow recommendations from a nutritionist
Can be based on actual calorie expenditure and weight change
cons: Requires knowledge of what constitutes realistic PAL values
Risk of incorrect estimates without experience
No guidance from the system
Easy to over- or underestimate
bestFor: Experienced users who know their actual PAL value
People following a nutritionist's recommendations
Those who have tracked calorie intake and weight changes over time
Advanced users who thoroughly understand PAL concepts

metabolicCalibration.title: Metabolic Calibration

foodLog.label: Calorie data: food log
foodLog.desc: You have logged food {{logged}} of {{total}} days ({{pct}}%). The calibration uses your actual logged intake.

blended.label: Calorie data: food log + weak prior correction
blended.estimatedAvg: Estimated average: {{kcal}} kcal/day (incl. weak correction for {{unlogged}} unlogged days)
blended.tip: Unlogged days reduce reliability — see Data quality below.

targetCalories.label: Calorie data: target calories
targetCalories.desc: You have not logged food sufficiently ({{logged}}/{{total}} days). The system assumes you ate according to your calorie range — if that is not the case, the calibration will be misleading.

metabolicCalibration.errorFallback: Try navigating backwards to find a period with data.

reasons.low_cluster_size: too few measurements in the start or end period ({{start}}+{{end}})
reasons.sparse_coverage: measurements are not spread across the full period
reasons.nonlinear_trend: weight signal is weak relative to daily variation — a longer period or more weight change is needed

high_cv.desc: High day-to-day weight variation is usually due to fluid fluctuations (salt, carbohydrates, exercise) rather than actual adipose tissue. It increases the residual variance in the trend calculation, which directly widens the confidence interval for the TDEE estimate. The warning is activated when:
bullets: The coefficient of variation (CV) for weight exceeds 2%
high_cv.tip: Tip: Weigh yourself every morning before breakfast under the same conditions — this is the single most effective way to reduce noise and get a narrower confidence interval.

target_calories_fallback.desc: If you actually ate more or less than the target, the calibration may be incorrect — it cannot see deviations that have not been logged. The warning is activated when:
bullets: No meals are logged during the period
target_calories_fallback.tip: Tip: Log your meals during the next period for a more reliable result.

selective_logging.desc: If you only log "good" days, calorie intake is underestimated and the calibration may suggest a TDEE that is too low. Currently:
selective_logging.tip: Tip: Try to log even the days you eat more — precision requires complete data.

glycogen_event.desc: Glycogen and fluid can cause weight changes of 1–3 kg on individual days without reflecting actual fat change. The trend calculation compensates but precision decreases. The warning is activated when:
bullets: A single daily weight change exceeds 1.5% of body weight
glycogen_event.tip: Tip: Calibration with a longer period (21–28 days) evens out temporary fluctuations better.

nonlinear_trend.desc: Weight has not moved in a steady direction during the period — likely a refeed period, diet break, or other transition. The warning is activated when any of the following apply:
bullets: The regression trend and EMA show clearly different total weight change
Weight moved in opposite directions during the two halves of the period with at least 1 kg total swing
nonlinear_trend.tip: Tip: Wait until weight has moved steadily in one direction for at least 2–3 weeks before calibrating.

large_deficit.desc: With a large deficit, the body adapts its metabolism downward, which means the calculated TDEE may underestimate your true maintenance requirement. The warning is activated when:
bullets: Calculated calorie deficit exceeds 25% of TDEE
large_deficit.tip: Tip: The result is likely an underestimate of your true TDEE — treat it as a floor rather than an exact value.

low_confidence.desc: The result is useful as a guideline but should be confirmed with a new calibration after more weeks of data. The warning is activated when any of the following apply:
bullets: Fewer than 3 weigh-ins per week on average
More than 70% of measurements are from the first half of the period
low_confidence.tip: Tip: Weigh yourself at least every other day and spread measurements evenly across the period.

large_adjustment.desc: Without the limit, the adjustment would have been {{diff}} kcal ({{raw}} kcal). Reason: {{reasons}}

reasons.dqiWasBindingCap: data quality score set an absolute cap

large_adjustment.tip: Tip: Improve data quality (more weigh-ins, complete logging) to allow larger adjustments in the future.

low_signal.desc: The change ({{change}} kg) is below the threshold ~{{threshold}} kg (0.25% of your weight). Either your TDEE is already correct, or fluid/glycogen is masking the true trend. When weight change is small relative to daily variation, noise dominates the signal — this widens the confidence interval and makes the TDEE estimate less reliable regardless of how long the period is.
low_signal.tip: Tip: If you are actively trying to lose or gain weight — wait another 1–2 weeks until the trend is clearer.

outlier_removed.tip: Tip: Outliers may be due to measurement error or unusual circumstances — they do not affect the calculation.

metabolicCalibration.stableMaintenance: Your weight is stable — your TDEE seems correct
metabolicCalibration.successDesc: Your calorie range has been updated
metabolicCalibration.revertConfirm: Restores TDEE to {{kcal}} kcal. Are you sure?

guardOverlap.title: The selected period window overlaps your last calibration — the same days would be counted again

guardNewWeights.title: {{count}} of {{required}} new weight measurements recorded since the last calibration end date

noDataState.line1: Needs at least {{min}} weight measurements over {{days}} days
noDataState.line2: Log more weight measurements to enable calibration

infoModal.section1p1: Metabolic calibration estimates your actual maintenance TDEE — how many calories your body burns to keep weight stable — by analysing how your body weight changes relative to your actual calorie intake over time.
infoModal.section1p2: Important to understand: The calibration estimates your actual energy expenditure, not your calorie goal. Your calorie range is then automatically recalculated based on your chosen energy goal (maintain weight, lose, gain). These are two separate steps.
infoModal.section1p3: Instead of relying solely on estimates from BMR formulas and activity levels, the system uses real data from your logging. This allows the calibration to capture individual differences that standard formulas miss — such as NEAT variation, thermogenesis, and metabolic adaptation.
infoModal.section2intro: The calibration is based on the energy balance principle:
infoModal.section2principle: Energy in − energy out = change in the body's energy stores.
infoModal.section2detail: If weight changes over time, we can estimate how large your actual energy requirement is. In simplified form:
infoModal.section2formula: TDEE ≈ Average calories − (weight change × kcal per kg / number of days)
infoModal.section2kcal: One kilogram of body weight is approximated to approximately 6,500–7,700 kcal. The model uses a dynamic value within this range based on rate of change — rapid changes typically contain more glycogen and fluid, slow changes more fat — but this is a model-based approximation, not an exact tissue analysis.
infoModal.section2clusterTitle: How start and end weight are determined
infoModal.section2cluster: The period is divided into a first and a last third, and the median of all measurements in each part is used as a robust anchor for the start and end weight respectively. It is these cluster medians that determine the weight levels in the calculation. Regression is then used for trend analysis, signal/noise assessment, and diagnostics — but does not change the start/end levels. This means that individual extreme weigh-ins cannot skew the result in the same way as if only a single measurement at each end had been used.
infoModal.section2calorie: The calibration uses your logged calorie intake as the primary data source. If not all days are logged, a weak statistical correction towards your calorie goal is applied — the more days without a log, the slightly greater the weight given to the calorie goal in the calculation. In practice, logged data almost always dominates.
infoModal.section2selective: The system also detects whether logging seems selective — that is, if you tend to only log days with lower intake. If that pattern is identified, the trust in logged data is reduced and the correction towards the calorie goal is adjusted. This protects against the calibration suggesting a TDEE that is too low based on biased data.
infoModal.section3Title: How the weight trend is calculated
infoModal.section3p1: Instead of comparing two individual weigh-ins, the system calculates a trendline (linear regression) through all weight measurements in the period. This gives a more stable estimate of the actual weight change and also measures how strong and consistent the trend is.
infoModal.section3p2: As a robustness check, an alternative trend is also calculated using the Theil–Sen estimator, which takes the median of all possible pairwise slopes between measurement points. It is more robust against individual outliers than linear regression. If the two methods show clearly different results, the system may warn that weight development was irregular during the period.
infoModal.section3p3: As an additional diagnostic, an exponentially smoothed trend (EMA) is calculated. It is not used as the primary calculation method but only to detect strongly non-linear weight development — e.g. a refeed period in the middle of the period.
infoModal.section3p4: Weight measurements that deviate greatly from the rest of the data are automatically filtered out before the trend calculation, to reduce the impact of individual extreme measurements.
infoModal.section4Title: How data quality affects the result
infoModal.section4p1: All calibrations receive a Data Quality Index (DQI) that assesses how reliable the data is. It is calculated from three factors:
section4bullets: Food log quality (45%) — the proportion of logged days and how consistently calories have been recorded
Weigh-in frequency (35%) — how regularly you have weighed yourself; 50% of days gives full score
Cluster size (20%) — how many measurements are in the start and end clusters of the period
infoModal.section4p2: DQI directly controls how large an adjustment is permitted: from ±75 kcal at low quality to ±200 kcal at high quality. This means better data not only gives a more reliable result — it also allows faster convergence towards your actual TDEE.
infoModal.section4tip: You can choose between 14, 21, and 28 days. Longer periods provide more data and allow slightly larger adjustments. Periods shorter than 14 days are not used — the signal-to-noise ratio becomes too weak to give reliable estimates, as daily weight fluctuations can be as large as the actual weight trend change.
infoModal.section5p1: The calibration limits how much TDEE can change in a single update. The maximum adjustment depends on data quality and normally lies between:
infoModal.section5p2: TDEE is never set below 1,200 or above 5,000 kcal regardless of what the calculation shows — these are absolute physiological limits. The clamp prevents temporary weight changes from causing unreasonable TDEE jumps.
infoModal.section5p3: If the calibration proposes a new TDEE level and the clamp is not triggered, the result is smoothly blended towards the last 1–3 previous calibrations. If all previous calibrations point in the same direction, the system follows that trend with minimal dampening instead of braking. If the clamp is triggered, the limited value is used directly without further smoothing.
infoModal.section5p4: Previous calibrations are used as a reference point but not indefinitely. Calibrations older than 90 days are excluded if data quality was low, and older than 180 days if quality was high. This means that if you have not calibrated for a long time, the system effectively restarts with minimal history influence.
infoModal.section6p1: Because real weight data contains variation, the model calculates a 90% confidence interval for the TDEE estimate. This means that if you repeated the same calibration with similar data, the true maintenance TDEE would fall within the interval in 90% of cases.
infoModal.section6p2: The width of the interval is calculated in three layers:
section6bullets: Residual variance (primary source) — how much weight fluctuates around the trendline from regression; this is the base uncertainty on which everything else is built
Calorie log uncertainty (widening factor) — estimated at ±20% of average calories, weighted against the number of logged days; added on top of the residual variance
Autocorrelation (widening factor) — daily weights are not independent of each other (a high measurement today likely affects tomorrow's), which further widens the interval with a correction factor
infoModal.section6p3: If body weight is very stable during the period, the confidence interval may be relatively wide. This is because daily weight variations from fluid, salt, and glycogen can then be as large as the actual trend change — the system simply has difficulty separating signal from noise. This is an honest answer to the data, not a sign that the calibration is wrong.
infoModal.section7Title: When should you use metabolic calibration?
infoModal.section7p1: Calibration works best when you have collected sufficient consistent data.
section7good: You have logged your calorie intake for at least 2–3 weeks
You weigh yourself regularly (ideally morning before breakfast)
You have logged the majority of days
Your weight is not developing as expected
infoModal.section8p1: Calibration can be misleading if the data does not represent a stable period.
section8bad: You have just started a new diet (<2 weeks)
You have recently significantly changed training volume
Weight measurements are missing for large parts of the period
infoModal.section9p1: Metabolic calibration is a long-term fine-tuning, not a quick fix.
infoModal.section9p2: Body weight is influenced daily by many factors not related to fat change. Therefore, the model is deliberately conservative and trend-based. It combines three separate robustness mechanisms:
section9bullets: Clustered start/end weight — median anchors at the period's extremes protect against individual extreme weigh-ins
Trend estimation over all data points — regression and Theil–Sen cross-check measure the trend without relying on two individual measurements
Data quality-weighted adjustment limit — DQI controls how large an adjustment is permitted, which prevents overreaction with uncertain data
infoModal.section9p3: It is precisely this combination that makes the model more stable than systems that only compare start and end weight or use simple rolling averages.
infoModal.section9p4: The more consistently you log food and weight over time, the more accurately the system can estimate your true energy requirements.
